cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are going deep into one of the most influential personal finance books ever written, Rich Dad Poor Dad by Robert Kiyosaki.
If you have ever felt like you work hard, you earn a decent living, and yet somehow the money never sticks around long enough for you to feel free, this book was written for you.

It has sold tens of millions of copies, and it has changed the way ordinary people think about money, not because it hands you a magic formula, but because it rewires the story in your head about what money even is and how it actually behaves.
So settle in.

Whether you are driving, walking, or grinding through a workout right now, I want you to listen closely, because the ideas in here are simple, but they run against almost everything you were taught, and once you see them, you cannot unsee them.
Let me start with the setup that gives the book its name, because everything flows from it.
Robert Kiyosaki grew up in Hawaii, and he tells you that he had two fathers.
Now, only one of them was his biological dad, but both of them shaped him profoundly, and the strange thing is that they gave him completely opposite advice about money.
His real father, the one he calls his poor dad, was a brilliant, highly educated man.
He had a PhD.
He was the head of education for the state of Hawaii.
He read books constantly, he valued academic achievement above almost everything, and he believed in the traditional path.
Go to school, get good grades, find a safe secure job with good benefits, work hard, and everything will take care of itself.
This was a man who was intelligent, respected, and hardworking, and yet he struggled with money his entire life.
He died leaving behind bills to be paid.
His other father, the man he calls rich dad, was the father of his best friend Mike.
And here is the twist.
Rich dad never finished the eighth grade.
He was, on paper, far less educated than Robert's real father.
But he had a gift for money, a way of thinking about it that was entirely his own, and over the course of his life he built a business empire.
He became one of the richest men in all of Hawaii, and when he died, he left behind tens of millions of dollars for his family, for charity, and for his church.
Two men.
Both worked hard.
Both were smart in their own ways.
One died broke, the other died wealthy.
And young Robert got to sit at the feet of both of them and listen to the way each of them talked about money.
That contrast is the whole engine of this book.
And it was not just that they earned different amounts.
It was that they said different things, opposite things, and those things planted opposite seeds in a young boy's mind.
Poor dad would say, I can't afford it.
Rich dad forbade those words.
He told Robert to ask instead, how can I afford it?
Do you hear the difference?
One is a statement that shuts your brain down.
The other is a question that forces your brain to open up and go to work.
Poor dad said the love of money is the root of all evil.
Rich dad said the lack of money is the root of all evil.
Poor dad said study hard so you can find a good company to work for.
Rich dad said study hard so you can find a good company to buy.
Poor dad believed the government should take care of people and that you should want security.
Rich dad believed in self reliance and thought that entitlement was what made people weak.
Robert listened to both, and slowly, deliberately, he chose to follow the thinking of the man who was actually good with money.
Not the man with the titles.
The man with the results.
Now here is the single sentence that echoes through the entire book, and I want you to really let it land.
The poor and the middle class work for money.
The rich have money work for them.
Read it again in your mind.
The poor and the middle class work for money.
The rich have money work for them.
That is the whole game, compressed into one line.
Most of us spend our entire lives on the first half of that sentence.
We trade our hours for dollars.
We get a job, we exchange our time and our energy for a paycheck, and then we do it again next week, and next month, and next year, for decades.
The rich, by contrast, spend their lives building or buying things that produce money whether they show up or not.
Their money goes to work so they do not have to.
And the difference between those two lives is not luck, and it is not even income.
It is a way of thinking that almost nobody teaches you, not in school, not at home, usually not anywhere.
Let me tell you the story of how rich dad first taught this lesson, because it is one of the best stories in the book, and it stuck with Robert his whole life.
When he was nine years old, Robert and his friend Mike decided they wanted to be rich.
They were just kids, so their first idea was almost comically literal.
They collected used toothpaste tubes, which back then were made of a soft metal, they melted the metal down in a steel milk carton over a fire, and they poured it into plaster molds.
They were, quite literally, trying to make money by counterfeiting nickels.
When Robert's real father saw what they were doing, he laughed and gently explained that this was illegal, and also that this was not how wealth actually worked.
But then he did the wise thing.
He told the boys to go ask Mike's father, rich dad, who had actually made money the real way.
And that is where the real education began.
The boys went to rich dad and said, teach us how to make money.
And rich dad agreed, but on his terms.
He hired them to work in one of his little convenience stores.
And here is the shock.
He paid them ten cents an hour.
Ten cents.
Even in the nineteen fifties, that was almost nothing.
The boys worked for a few weeks, dusting cans, showing up on Saturdays, and Robert got angrier and angrier.
Finally he had enough.
He marched up to rich dad, ready to quit, and he complained.
He said this is not fair, you are exploiting us, you are not teaching us anything, you are just using us for cheap labor.
And rich dad, instead of getting defensive, smiled.
He said, that is exactly the lesson.
That anger you feel right now, that is what most people feel their whole lives and never understand.
Rich dad explained that the ten cents an hour was a trap he set on purpose, and it was designed to teach the most important lesson of all.
Most people, he said, go through life exactly like this.
They feel underpaid, they feel it is not fair, they blame the boss, they blame the company, they blame the economy, and that blaming, that anger, keeps them stuck.
It keeps them a victim.
Because as long as you are blaming someone else for your financial situation, you have given away all of your power.
You are waiting for someone else to pay you what you think you deserve, and you will wait forever.
Rich dad said the poor and the middle class let two emotions run their lives.
Fear and greed.
Fear of not having money drives them to take a job, and then when the paycheck arrives, greed and desire make them think about all the wonderful things money can buy.
And so they spend it.
And then the fear comes back, because now the money is gone, and they need the next paycheck.
And they go right back to work.
He called this the rat race.
Get up, go to work, pay the bills, get up, go to work, pay the bills.
Around and around, driven by fear and desire, never stopping long enough to ask whether there is another way.
And then rich dad taught the boys the real lesson.
He said, I do not want you to work for money.
I want you to learn how to make money work for you.
He stopped paying them altogether.
He had them work for free, which sounds even worse, but it forced their brains to wake up.
If you are not being paid, you have to get creative, you have to find opportunity, you have to think like an owner instead of an employee.
And the boys did.
Robert noticed that the store threw away the old comic books, tearing the covers off to return them to the distributor.
He got permission to take the coverless comics, and he and Mike set up a little library in Mike's basement.
They charged the neighborhood kids ten cents to come in and read as many comics as they wanted for two hours after school.
They hired Mike's sister to run it and paid her a dollar a week.
Dozens of kids came.
The boys were making money without even being there.
That was the point rich dad wanted them to feel in their bones.
The comic book library made money whether Robert was in the room or not.
He had built, for the first time in his life, an asset.
Money was working for him.
And that feeling, that little taste of freedom, is the seed the entire book tries to plant in you.
So let me get to the heart of the whole thing, the one rule rich dad said was the most important of all, the rule he said you could build a fortune on if you truly understood it.
You must know the difference between an asset and a liability, and you must buy assets.
That is it.
Rich people acquire assets.
The poor and middle class acquire liabilities that they think are assets.

Now the definitions Robert uses are deliberately simple, so simple that accountants sometimes argue with him, but the simplicity is the point, because it is something a nine year old could understand and act on.
An asset is something that puts money in your pocket.
A liability is something that takes money out of your pocket.
That is the whole test.
When you are looking at anything you own or anything you are about to buy, ask one question.
Does this put money into my pocket, or does it take money out.
If it feeds you, it is an asset.
If it eats, it is a liability.
This sounds almost too obvious, but watch how it dismantles one of the most cherished beliefs in the modern world.
Rich dad said your house is not an asset.
Say what now.
Yes.
Your primary home, the one you live in, the one people call the biggest investment of their lives, in most cases is not an asset at all.
It is a liability.
Why?
Go back to the test.
Does your house put money in your pocket every month, or does it take money out.
For most people, the house takes money out.
The mortgage payment, the property taxes, the insurance, the utilities, the repairs, the new roof, the maintenance, all of it flows out of your pocket month after month.
The house does not pay you.
You pay the house.
Now, Robert is not saying never own a home, and he is not saying homes never go up in value.
His point is deeper.

He is saying that when you call your house your biggest asset while it is actually draining your cash flow every single month, you have confused yourself about the most fundamental distinction in all of personal finance, and that confusion is exactly why so many educated, hardworking people never build wealth.
They think they are accumulating assets when they are really accumulating liabilities with nicer names.
This is where financial literacy comes in, and it is a theme rich dad hammered again and again.
Robert argues that the reason smart people struggle with money is that they are financially illiterate.
They were never taught to read the language of money, the language of accounting and numbers.
Now do not tune out at the word accounting, because this is simpler than you fear, and it might be the most valuable ninety seconds of this whole episode.
Rich dad drew simple diagrams for the boys, a box for income, a box for expenses, a box for assets, and a box for liabilities.
And he showed them that the difference between a rich person, a middle class person, and a poor person is not how much money flows in.
It is where the money flows.
He called this cash flow, and he said if you can learn to read the direction the money is flowing, you can see a person's entire financial future in a glance.
Let me paint the three patterns for you, because once you see them you will recognize them everywhere, in your friends, in your family, maybe in yourself.
The cash flow pattern of a poor person is the simplest.
Money comes in as income, and it goes straight out to expenses.
Rent, food, bills, gone.
There is nothing left, no assets, no investments, just income in and expenses out, month after month.
They are surviving, and it is exhausting, but at least it is straightforward.
Now the middle class.
This is the one that will sting, because this is where most people listening probably live, and it is the most seductive trap of all.
The middle class person earns more, sometimes a lot more.
But here is the pattern.
Their income comes in, and instead of buying assets, they buy liabilities that look like success.
A bigger house with a bigger mortgage.
A newer car with a monthly payment.
Credit card balances for the vacations and the furniture and the lifestyle.
And so their expenses grow right alongside their income, and their liability column swells, and the money that flows in flows right back out to service all of that debt.
This is the trap of the raise.
You get a promotion, you earn more, and instead of using that extra money to buy assets, you upgrade your lifestyle.
You buy the bigger house, you take on the bigger payment, you acquire more liabilities.
And so the raise does not make you free.
It actually makes you more trapped, because now you need that higher income even more than before to keep the whole thing from collapsing.
Rich dad called this the rat race, and the cruel irony is that the harder these people work and the more they earn, the deeper into it they run.
More money in often just means more liabilities, and the fear of losing it all keeps them chained to the job.
It is a beautiful cage, but it is still a cage.
And then the rich.
Here is the pattern that changes everything.
The rich person focuses relentlessly on one column, the asset column.
They buy or build assets, things that generate income.
Real estate that pays rent.
Businesses that run without them.
Stocks that pay dividends.
Notes, royalties, intellectual property.
And those assets generate income.
And here is the beautiful part.
That income from the assets is then used to buy more assets.
The assets buy more assets.
The money makes more money.
And over time the asset column grows so large that the income it produces covers all of the person's expenses, and at that moment, that magic moment, they are financially free.
They no longer have to work, because their assets work for them.
Their money has employees, and those employees never sleep, never take a vacation, and never quit.
So the whole strategy of the book, if you had to boil it down to one instruction, is this.
Spend your life buying assets.
Keep your liabilities and expenses low.
And funnel every dollar you can into the asset column until the income from your assets is greater than all of your expenses.
That is the finish line.
That is what it means to be rich in Kiyosaki's world.
It is not about a huge salary.
Wealth, he says, is measured in time.
It is the number of days you could survive without working.
If you stopped earning today, how long could your assets keep you alive.
That number is your true wealth, and it has almost nothing to do with your paycheck.
This leads directly into one of the most memorable phrases in the book.
Mind your own business.
Now rich dad meant something very specific and a little bit cheeky by this.
He said that most people spend their whole lives minding someone else's business.
You work all day building the company you are employed by.
You make the owner rich, you make the shareholders rich, and at the end of the day you have built someone else's asset column, not your own.
Your profession, your job, that is not your business.
Your business is your asset column.
So when he says mind your own business, he means keep your day job if you need it, that is fine, but do not confuse your job with your business.
In your spare time, with your spare money, tend to your own asset column.
Buy the real estate.
Start the side venture.
Acquire the stocks.
Build the thing that belongs to you and produces income for you.
Because if you spend forty years minding the boss's business and never tend your own, you will retire with nothing but memories of hard work.
He tells a wonderful story to illustrate this, one involving Ray Kroc, the man who built McDonald's into a global empire.
As the story goes, Kroc was speaking to a class of MBA students, and he asked them, what business am I in.
And they laughed, because obviously the answer was hamburgers.
Kroc said no.
He said, my business is not hamburgers.
My business is real estate.
And he was serious.

McDonald's, one of the most valuable brands on earth, understood that the hamburger business was really the engine that let them acquire and control some of the most valuable street corners and intersections in every town in the world.
The burgers paid the bills, but the real wealth, the enduring asset, was the land beneath the restaurants.
That is minding your own business.
The visible business is one thing.
The asset column underneath it is what makes you rich.
Now we come to one of the most eye opening and, honestly, one of the most controversial sections of the book.

Rich dad taught Robert about the history and power of the corporation, and how the truly wealthy use corporations and the tax code and legal structures to protect and grow their money in ways the average employee never gets to.
And I want to be careful here, because I am not giving you legal or tax advice, and the specifics of tax law change and vary by country.
But the principle Kiyosaki is teaching is about mindset and awareness, and it is genuinely worth understanding.
He describes it as a difference in the order of operations.
The employee earns money, then the government taxes that money before the employee ever sees it, and then the employee spends whatever is left.
Earn, taxed, spend.
The tax is taken right off the top, out of every paycheck, automatically, before you can do anything about it.
The rich, operating through corporations, follow a different sequence.
The corporation earns money, then it spends money on legitimate business expenses, and then it pays taxes on whatever is left over.
Earn, spend, then taxed.
Do you see the difference?
By legally running expenses through the business structure before the tax is calculated, the wealthy control the timing and the amount of what they are taxed on in ways an employee simply cannot.

Kiyosaki even tells the story that the corporation, historically, was invented in part as a legal vehicle to limit the risk and protect the assets of the wealthy, going back to the days of sailing ventures where investors wanted to risk only the money they put into a single voyage and not their entire personal fortune.
His larger point is that financial intelligence includes knowing the rules of the game.

The rich are not necessarily smarter than you, but they know things about how money, taxes, corporations, and the law fit together that were never taught to you, and that knowledge is a form of power.
He is not telling you to break the rules.
He is telling you to learn them, because the people who know the rules use them, and the people who do not know the rules pay for that ignorance their whole lives.
Rich dad said that financial intelligence rests on four broad areas of knowledge, and if you want to master money, you invest in learning all four.
The first is accounting, which he called financial literacy, the ability to read numbers and understand the story a set of financial statements is telling you.
The second is investing, the science of money making money, understanding strategies and how assets grow.
The third is understanding markets, the science of supply and demand, knowing what people want and how to read the conditions around you.
And the fourth is the law, understanding the tax advantages and the legal protections and corporate structures we just talked about.
Master those four, he said, and you have the foundation of real financial genius.
And notice that none of the four is get a high paying job.
Not one.
The whole framework is about building the intelligence to make money work for you, not about working harder for money.
But knowing all of this is not enough, and Kiyosaki is refreshingly honest about that.
He says that even people who become financially literate, even people who understand assets and liabilities and cash flow, often still fail to build wealth.
Why?
Because there are five obstacles, five personal traits that stand between financial knowledge and actual financial success, and he devotes a whole section to them.
I want to walk you through all five, because I promise you will recognize at least one of them in yourself, and naming it is the first step to beating it.
The first obstacle is fear.
Specifically, the fear of losing money.
Everybody has it.
There is no investor on earth who loves losing money.
But the difference between the rich and everyone else is how they handle that fear.

Most people are so terrified of losing that they never take the risk in the first place, so they play it safe, they keep everything in a savings account earning almost nothing, and they lose slowly and safely to inflation instead of risking a loss on the way to a gain.
Rich dad taught that if you hate the very idea of risk, if you want guarantees, you will pay a huge price in the long run.
He taught Robert to see failure not as the opposite of success but as a part of it.
He said losing is part of winning.
Nobody becomes wealthy without losing some money along the way.
The Texans, he said, have the right attitude.
Remember the Alamo.
They lost that battle, but they turned the loss into a rallying cry.
Winners are not afraid of losing, but losers are.
Failure is part of the process.
If you are so afraid of losing that you never even start, you have guaranteed the one outcome you were trying to avoid.
The second obstacle is cynicism.
The little voice of doubt, and worse, the loud voices of the doubters around you.
Rich dad called them the Chicken Littles, the people who run around shouting the sky is falling, the sky is falling.
When you get excited about an opportunity, there is always someone, sometimes inside your own head, sometimes a friend or a relative, who will tell you all the reasons it will not work.
What if you lose your money.
What if the market crashes.
What if it does not work out.
And here is the sneaky thing about cynicism.
It sounds like wisdom.
It sounds smart and cautious and responsible.
But often it is just fear dressed up in a suit, and it stops people from ever taking action.
Kiyosaki points out that these doubts and criticisms often keep the cynics themselves poor, and worse, the successful investor uses the cynicism of the crowd to his advantage.
When everyone is too scared and too cynical to buy, that is often exactly when the bargains appear.
Do not let the Chicken Littles, whether they live in your neighborhood or in your own mind, talk you out of your future.
The third obstacle is laziness, but Kiyosaki gives it a twist that will make you uncomfortable in the best way.
He says the most common form of laziness is being busy.
Yes, you heard that right.
Being busy can be a form of laziness.

Think about the person who stays late at the office, buries themselves in work, tells everyone how slammed they are, and uses all of that busyness as an excuse to avoid dealing with the things that actually matter.
They are too busy to look at their finances.
Too busy to learn about investing.
Too busy to spend time with their family.
Too busy to take care of their health.
The busyness becomes a way of hiding from the important questions.
And Kiyosaki's cure for this kind of laziness is one word.
Greed, or as he frames it more gently, a little bit of healthy wanting.
He says to ask yourself, what is in it for me.
Let yourself dream about what you actually want, the freedom, the trips, the time, the security for your family.
Let that desire pull you forward and give you the energy to stop being busy with the wrong things and start being active with the right ones.
The fourth obstacle is bad habits.
And here he tells you about the single most powerful money habit there is, one so important it becomes a theme all its own.
Pay yourself first.
Now think about how most people operate.
When the paycheck comes in, they pay everyone else first.
They pay the landlord, the credit card company, the phone company, the government, and then, if there is anything left over, which there usually is not, they save a little for themselves.
Rich dad flipped this completely.
He said pay yourself first.
Before you pay a single bill, take a portion of your income and put it into your asset column.
Invest it.
Save it.
Move it out of reach.
Pay yourself before you pay anyone else.
And Kiyosaki knows this sounds financially reckless, so listen closely to the psychology, because this is the genius of it.
When you pay yourself first, you deliberately create pressure on yourself.
Now there is less money to cover the bills, and that pressure, that discomfort, does not make you go raid your investments.
Instead it forces your brain to get creative, to find ways to earn more, to hustle, to generate additional income to cover the shortfall.
The pressure to pay the tax man and the other creditors becomes the fuel that pushes you to become smarter and more resourceful about money.
Weak people, he says, give in to the pressure and pay themselves last.
Strong people use the pressure to grow.
Pay yourself first, even when it is tight, especially when it is tight, because that is when the discipline matters most.
And the fifth obstacle is arrogance.
Rich dad defined arrogance as ego plus ignorance.
It is the trap of thinking you already know everything, of being too proud to admit what you do not know.
He said that whenever he felt arrogant about a subject, especially money, it was usually a signal that he was about to lose.
Because arrogance closes your mind.
When you think you know it all, you stop learning, you stop asking questions, and you make expensive mistakes based on things you were too proud to find out.
The cure for arrogance is humility and education.
When you know you do not know something, find an expert, read a book, take a course, ask questions.
Never let your ego write checks that your ignorance cannot cash.
There is another huge idea in this book that runs against everything most of us were taught about careers, and it might be the single most practical piece of advice in the whole thing.
Work to learn, do not work to earn.
Kiyosaki tells a story about being a young man, already a published writer with a bestselling book, when a journalist asked him for advice on how she could become a better selling author herself.
And he told her she should take a job in sales, maybe at a place like a car dealership, to learn how to sell.
She was offended.
She had a master's degree in English literature, she considered herself a professional, and the idea of taking a sales job felt like an insult, like a step down.
And Robert pointed out the irony.
She wanted to be a bestselling author, and the word she kept getting stuck on was selling.
Bestselling.
The skill she was missing was not writing.
It was selling.
And she was too proud to go learn it.
Here is the principle.
When you are young, and honestly at any age, choose your work not for the paycheck but for what it will teach you.
Seek jobs that build skills, especially the skills that make you money over a lifetime.
And the biggest ones, Kiyosaki says again and again, are sales, marketing, and communication.
Learn to sell.
Learn to market.
Learn to communicate with different kinds of people, to speak, to write, to negotiate.
These are the skills that separate the wealthy from the merely educated.
His own poor dad believed in specializing, in becoming more and more expert in a narrow field, which is how you climb the ladder as an employee.
But rich dad believed in the opposite.

He believed you should know a little about a lot, that you should learn many different skills, and that you should be willing to take a lower paying job or even work for free if it teaches you something valuable, because the skills you build will pay you back for the rest of your life in ways a slightly higher salary never could.
So the next time you are weighing an opportunity, do not just ask what it pays.
Ask what it teaches.
Because at the start of your journey to wealth, what you learn is worth far more than what you earn.
Underneath all of these lessons runs a psychological truth that Kiyosaki keeps returning to, and I want to make sure you really hear it, because it is the thing most money books ignore.
Money decisions are not logical.
They are emotional.
Two emotions in particular govern almost everyone's financial life.
Fear and greed, or you could call it fear and desire.
Fear of not having enough drives us to grab a job and hold onto it for dear life.
And then desire, the wanting, pulls us to spend what we earn on all the things we believe will make us happy.
And so we swing back and forth between fear and desire our whole lives, and that swinging is what keeps us running in the rat race.
The person who wins with money is not the one with the highest IQ or the fanciest degree.

It is the person who learns to master their own emotions, who can feel the fear and act intelligently anyway, who can feel the desire and still delay the gratification and buy the asset instead of the toy.

If you cannot control your emotions around money, all the financial knowledge in the world will not save you, because in the heat of the moment, when the market is crashing or when the shiny thing is right in front of you, emotion will override logic every single time unless you have trained yourself.
So part of getting rich is inner work.
It is developing the self awareness to notice when fear or greed is driving the bus, and taking the wheel back.
Now, I want to be honest with you about what this book is and what it is not, because that honesty is part of taking it seriously.
Rich Dad Poor Dad is not a step by step instruction manual.
It will not tell you exactly which stock to buy or precisely how to structure a real estate deal or which specific account to open on Tuesday morning.
It is not that kind of book, and if you go in expecting a paint by numbers blueprint, you will be frustrated.
What it is, and what it does better than almost any book ever written, is change your mindset.
It is a financial wake up call.

It takes the beliefs you absorbed without ever choosing them, the belief that a good job equals security, that your house is your greatest asset, that debt is normal, that you should study hard only so you can work for someone else, and it holds each one up to the light and asks, is that actually true.
And once those beliefs crack, you start to see money differently, permanently.
That shift in seeing is the real gift of this book.
The specific tactics you can learn anywhere.
The change in how you think is what stays with you.
People do criticize the book, and it is fair to know that.
Some point out that the two dads may be more parable than literal biography.
Some argue that his definition of an asset is too loose for a professional accountant.
Some say the book is light on concrete how to.
And all of that can be true, and the book can still be one of the most valuable things you ever put into your mind, because its job was never to be a technical manual.

Its job was to make you question the automatic script, to make a nine year old's simple question, does this put money in my pocket or take it out, echo in your head every time you reach for your wallet for the rest of your life.
Judged by that standard, it succeeds enormously.
And there is one more theme I promised we would come back to, and it might be the most important of all, because it reframes the entire goal.
It is not how much money you make.
It is how much money you keep, and how hard it works for you, and how many generations you can keep it.
Read that again.
It is not how much you make.
It is how much you keep.
You can know people who earn enormous salaries and are broke, living paycheck to paycheck at a higher altitude, one bad month from disaster, because they spend everything they make and then some.
And you can know people of modest income who quietly build real wealth, because they keep what they earn, they buy assets, and they let those assets compound over years and decades.
The scoreboard was never your salary.
The scoreboard is your net worth, your asset column, the cash flow that keeps coming whether you work or not.
And the truly wealthy think even further out than their own lifetime.
They think about keeping wealth across generations, building something that outlives them and gives their children a head start their parents never had.
That is the long game, and it starts with the very first asset you choose to buy.
So let me bring all of this together before we get to what you are going to do this week.
Two dads, one who worked for money and stayed poor despite his brilliance, and one who made money work for him and grew rich despite his lack of formal education.
The whole difference between them lived in how they thought.
The poor and middle class work for money, the rich have money work for them.
Know the difference between an asset and a liability, and pour your life into buying assets.
Understand that your house eating your cash every month is not the asset you were told it was.
Recognize the cash flow patterns, and see how the raise, the bigger house, the nicer car, the extra liabilities, quietly tighten the trap of the rat race.
Mind your own business, tend your own asset column, do not spend your whole life building someone else's.
Learn the rules of corporations, taxes, and the law, so you play the game with your eyes open.
Build the four pillars of financial intelligence, accounting, investing, markets, and law.
Beat the five obstacles, fear, cynicism, laziness, bad habits, and arrogance.
Pay yourself first, even when it is tight, and let that pressure make you resourceful.
Work to learn, not just to earn, and chase skills like sales, marketing, and communication over a slightly bigger paycheck.
Master your own emotions, because fear and greed, not logic, are what really move money.
And never forget, it is not what you make, it is what you keep, and how long you keep it.
Now, none of this matters if you close this book, or turn off this episode, and go right back to living the exact same way.
So let me give you a concrete action list, things you can actually do this week, starting today, to begin turning these ideas into your reality.
First, this week, write down your own personal financial statement, by hand, the simple way rich dad taught it.
Four boxes.
Income, expenses, assets, and liabilities.
List everything honestly.
And then go down the list of things you own and ask the one question, does this put money in my pocket, or does it take money out.
Sort them ruthlessly into assets and liabilities.
Most people have never once seen their money laid out this way, and just doing this will show you truths you have been avoiding.
Second, set up a pay yourself first system this week.
Decide on a percentage, even if it is small, even if it is just five or ten percent of what comes in, and automate it.

Set up an automatic transfer so that the moment money hits your account, a portion moves into a separate account earmarked for buying assets, before you can spend it, before you can even think about it.
Make it automatic so your willpower is not part of the equation.
Third, commit to your financial education starting now.
Rich dad said your mind is your greatest asset, so feed it.
This week, pick one book on investing or money, or one course, or start following one credible teacher, and begin.
Block out time, even thirty minutes a few times a week, to learn about one of the four areas, accounting, investing, markets, or the law.
You are building financial intelligence, and it compounds just like money does.
Fourth, identify one liability you are currently paying for that is draining you, and make a plan to reduce or eliminate it.
Maybe it is a subscription you forgot about, maybe it is high interest debt eating your cash flow, maybe it is a lifestyle expense that looked like success but is really just an anchor.
Free up that cash, and redirect it toward your asset column.
Fifth, take one honest look at the five obstacles, and name the one that has the biggest grip on you.
Is it fear of losing money, so you never start.
Is it cynicism, letting the doubters win.
Is it laziness disguised as busyness.
Is it the bad habit of paying yourself last.
Or is it arrogance, thinking you already know enough.
Name it out loud.
Then take one small action this week that pushes directly against it.
If it is fear, make one tiny investment.
If it is busyness, block one hour for your finances.
If it is arrogance, admit one thing you do not know and go learn it.
And sixth, make the mind your own business commitment.
Keep your job if you need it, no shame in that, but decide right now that you are going to start building something of your own on the side, your own asset column.
This week, just research one thing.

One type of income producing asset, whether it is a first small investment, a side venture, a skill you could sell, anything that belongs to you and could one day put money in your pocket whether you show up or not.
You do not have to buy it this week.
You just have to begin.
Here is the truth I want to leave you with.
Robert Kiyosaki's rich dad was not born rich, and he barely finished school.
What he had was a different way of thinking, and he chose it, deliberately, every single day.
You have that same choice available to you right now, in this moment, wherever you are, driving, walking, lifting.
You can keep working for money for the rest of your life, or you can begin, starting today, to make money work for you.
It starts small.
It starts with one financial statement, one automatic transfer, one book, one asset.
But it starts with a decision, and the decision is yours.
So make it.
Your future self, the one who is free, is counting on the choice you make today.
Go build your asset column.
Go mind your own business.
And remember, it is not how much you make.
It is how much you keep.
Now go get started.
